prompt: Write a poem about a lighthouse keeper named Orvath. Hard constraints: (1) exactly 5 lines, no blank lines; (2) every line contains exactly 7 words, separated by single spaces, with no leading or trailing spaces (punctuation attached to a word counts as part of that word); (3) every line ends with a period; (4) the word 'lantern' appears at least once; (5) the word 'sea' does not appear anywhere, in any casing. Output ONLY the poem - no title, no commentary.

Describe a midnight robbery of a bank vault in exactly 3 sentences. Hard constraints: (1) exactly 3 sentences, each ending with a period; (2) the letter 'e' (uppercase or lowercase) must not appear anywhere in your reply - a full lipogram; (3) the word 'vault' must appear; (4) the word 'moon' must appear. Output ONLY the 3-sentence description - no preamble, no commentary.

Write exactly 4 lines of verse about walking through a city at night. Hard constraints: (1) exactly 4 lines, no blank lines; (2) every line begins with the word 'Under' - capital U, the rest lowercase, followed by a single space; (3) the third word of every line is exactly 'silver' (all lowercase, no punctuation attached); (4) every line contains at least 6 space-separated words; (5) no commas anywhere. Output ONLY the 4 lines - no title, no commentary.

Consider these five words: plinth, caravel, mist, obsidian, fern. Step 1: sort them by word length in DESCENDING order; break any length ties alphabetically (ascending A-Z). Step 2: convert every word to uppercase. Step 3: join the words with single hyphens, no spaces. Reply with ONLY the resulting string - no quotes, no explanation, no punctuation other than the hyphens.

Write a poem about a harbor at dusk, structured as exactly 3 stanzas separated by single blank lines, where each stanza has exactly 2 lines (6 lines total). Hard constraints: (1) the final word of every line ends with the letters 'ight' (examples: night, light, flight, sight) and has NO punctuation or spaces after it - each line ends with the bare word; (2) the word 'moon' appears exactly once in the whole poem (any casing); (3) no blank lines inside a stanza. Output ONLY the poem - no title, no commentary.
